summary: Jeff Atwood argues that users consistently misrepresent their actual behavior, buying products for features they'll never use and claiming to love software they actually find frustrating. Drawing on Heidi Adkisson's research showing users aspire to use premium features but default to basics, he connects this to the 1987 'Paradox of the Active User' concept. Atwood invokes the TV character Dr. House's maxim 'Everybody lies' to explain that users lie primarily to themselves about their intentions and capabilities. The conclusion is that software designers must observe actual user behavior rather than trust self-reported preferences, since actions—especially those involving money—reveal truth more reliably than words.
key_points: Users buy products for aspirational features they never actually use, paying premiums for capabilities they'll never invest time to learn
The 'Paradox of the Active User' explains that users refuse to read manuals or learn systems properly, even when it would save them time long-term
People lie not from malice but from self-deception—their stated intentions diverge wildly from their actual behavior
Behavioral observation, not user surveys or feedback, should drive software design decisions
Economic thinking and studying actual transactions reveals truth because actions involving money are the most carefully considered
money_quotes: Ultimately, their aspirations may be different than the reality.
Observation is a powerful skill, and so is learning to disregard what people tell you in favor of judging them by their actions.
No actions are more carefully considered than those that result in money flowing out of your pocket.
People lie not because they're all evil liars, but because they're usually lying to themselves in some way.
Users never read manuals but start using the software immediately.
We must design for the way users actually behave.
Observe whether or not they use the software, and how they use it. Rely on that behavioral data to design your software, and not the lies of your users, however well intentioned they may be.
key_insight: Design software based on observed user behavior, not stated preferences, because users consistently deceive themselves about how they'll actually use products.
